messages.content:
Here is the conversation transcript between Joe, John and Varun (that is me). The context of the below conversation is as follows:
Context: Non-functional team (NFT) and Accessibility Centre of Excellence (ACoE) team need to collaborate as ACoE team want to setup the Centre of Excellence within an organisation. NFT team is responsible for providing various testers to the project team including accessibility testers.

Conversation Transcript:
John: Hi Joe, You was going back up the management chain to get a view on the overarching NFT team covering the gap between the level of assurance provided from a project handed to the CoE.
 
Joe: We spoke with Struan about the A11y Testers on the most part being a pool based out of NFT.  I think we have agreement in principle.  We did agree that most agile squads would need an A11y Tester permanently embedded in a squad - this would be required to truly shift left on RLGs A11y compliance as a whole.  I believe it will become clearer with time just how many A11y Testers will be required.  I suspect you guys are probably a bit optimistic on the number and the ACoE is pessimistic on the number (i.e. need too many)  - let's not worry about the specifics of that at the moment.
Varun and I are still actively building out the Roadmap for ACoE and have made good headway.  We will come back to you once we've got that and can share plans - Next week is my guess.
 
John: Embedding a specific Accessibility tester into each squad might not be the right approach. We are just ready to roll out a guide to what the performance team service can offer which can be used for Accessibility. Manage - Build, run and maintain the performance test suite, Support - Help build the test suite, provide guidance to the team/project on how to take things forward, Consult - the team/project are skilled up to build, run and maintain their performance suite. If a squad brings in a skilled accessibility tester to build, run and maintain their test suite. Once complete, they will have an expensive resource, at best, doing a side role when there is not accessibility testing to do. What the NFT team is offering is to engage with the team, provide hands on when needed, support through the process of becoming compliant and providing training/upskilling to whoever wants to learn on an ongoing basis.
 
Joe: I've asked Varun Padiyar if he is able to work this through. Varun, I think it would be helpful if we could have visibility of the guide John is talking about.  Can you please send this to us John White? One note before I let Varun continue ... I dare say that you may be over simplifying by saying "Build, run and maintain" the suite.  Yes, automation sort of runs itself once it is set up, and people are paying attention to the results.  About 50% of Accessibility testing is manual testing and will be ongoing throughout the life of a product.  We need to set ourselves up so that each agile squad has the ability to carry out necessary manual A11y testing for each of their work items in their sprints.  Otherwise they will try to release a product that has not been fully A11y tested.
 
John:
Hi Joe, when I say maintain, I'm talking about the whole process. For each manual test, there should be a script that is followed so the test is repeatable, there should be knowledge transfer and upskilling, and anything that is automated there should a maintenance wrapper around it. The whole test piece in this area should have the relevant attention paid to it as you would expect with anything as critical, carrying such a potential impact on credibility, as Accessibility. I think we might be getting our wires crossed here. Also, we need to make sure that change which falls into a waterfall'ish type of bucket is also covered.
 
Varun: Hi John, thanks for sharing your thoughts. It’s great to have your expertise as we figure out how to best integrate non-functional testing services within our teams and ACoE.
I wanted to touch on why we might really benefit from having someone focus just on accessibility within each team:
1. Accessibility is specialised and having someone dedicated means we’re always on the ball with the latest guidelines.
2. With a dedicated tester, we can make sure accessibility gets the continuous attention it needs, which is crucial.
3. When we’re working quickly in Agile, having an expert around means we can sort any issues out straight away.
4. If someone’s trying to handle both accessibility and performance testing, they might not be able to give each area enough attention. Accessibility needs a full-time eye.
5. A tester who knows accessibility inside out will always keep our users’ needs at the forefront, making sure our products are inclusive.
Now, regarding the Waterfall projects, I fully recognise that their structure is more linear than Agile. However, the benefits of having a dedicated accessibility tester are just as important, if not more so. Waterfall projects can sometimes silo different phases such as design, development, and testing. A dedicated accessibility tester ensures that at each stage, accessibility requirements are clearly defined and met before moving to the next phase. This minimises the risk of discovering major accessibility issues late in the project when they are more costly and time-consuming to fix.
Having someone consistently championing accessibility throughout the Waterfall process embeds it into the project from the very beginning. This helps prevent any last-minute surprises that could delay project timelines and ensures a high-quality product for all users.
Let’s keep working together to create a process that supports both Agile and Waterfall methods, helping RLG lead the way in inclusive design.
 
John: Cheers for the above Varun, much appreciated. We might be tackling this in the wrong way. We shouldn't be thinking of working in a waterfall or agile way, Accessibility needs to be embedded in the work to be done from the start and, as we agreed, that needs the right lens applying. Embedding 'Accessibility' from the earliest point is key.
 
Varun: Yes John, completely agree with your thought. We always recommend that accessibility should be included right from the "ideation" phase.
 
John: The framework looks to put the onerous on project to get to a point in time where they have completed their assurance work, then sending this to the CoE to validate. This has a potential to create a bottle neck.Aside from that, embedding an Accessibility SME tester to each team/squad/project would not only be very costly but inefficient. A change could have no impact on Accessibility. The expensive SME would either be sat doing nothing or providing support for work that is below their pay grade. I'm not saying that an SME shouldn't be budgeted for, I'm saying it needs to be thought about and cost effective, which is something I've done on the performance area of NFT.
 
John: On Tuesday. myself and Varun had a conversation around if there was a need for the NFT team to provide any support in the process that change goes through on it's path to live and ticking all the boxes for Accessibility. Points captured from the meeting on Tuesday: -
1. Agreed that there is a skills gap at the moment with the understanding of Accessibility that teams and projects have, and what needs to be done to be compliant
2. This has the potential to impact project go live
3. Change being delivered in both agile and waterfall methodologies need to be compliant, they both need to be thought about and engage in the process with the ACoE
4. The hand over process and impact needs to be worked through
5. When will a project have to complete their Accessibility artefacts and provide them to the ACoE?
6. If this point is a large amount of time before go live this will impact agile projects
7. If this is small amount of time before go live, if major issues are encountered that require a lot of effort and time to fix, this will impact go live
8. Having a number of Accessibility SMEs in a small team, responsible for NFT, would significantly mitigated the above risks
9. To make this work, who ever is delivering this change should make appropriate adjustments to their budget at the relevant phase
The next step is to work out the engagement process that all parties should follow. This will identify any touch points where there might be friction. Varun, please add to this if you think I have missed anything.
 
Varun: Thanks John. These basically sums up our conversation. I would also like to add some additional points which can be useful:
1. Regarding the responsibility for ensuring accessibility implementation, it is important to clarify that while the ACoE governs accessibility in the organization, the ultimate responsibility lies with the project team. The ACoE is there to provide guidance, support, and ensure compliance, but it is the project team’s responsibility to implement accessibility correctly.
2. If at any point it becomes evident that the project go-live is being impacted by the ACoE process , the project team has the option to raise a deferment request. This request would allow them to proceed with the go-live while ensuring that any accessibility issues are addressed promptly in a future release.

Analyze the above conversation thread and provide your opinion.

The above conversation indicates a thoughtful and collaborative effort between the Non-functional team (NFT), the Accessibility Centre of Excellence (ACoE), and project teams to solidify practices for integrating accessibility testing into the organization’s software development life cycle (SDLC).

From the transcript, it's evident that both Joe and John understand the importance of accessibility as well as the challenges in operationalizing it within agile and waterfall teams. Varun's input reflects the need for dedicated accessibility expertise within each team and emphasizes the continuous nature of accessibility work. Everyone seems focused on creating a system that ensures compliance without hindering the development process.

**Joe's Points:**
- He acknowledges the need for dedicated A11y Testers in agile squads to meet RLG's compliance standards.
- Joe points out the need for manual testing in accessibility and the implication for ongoing team involvement.
- He requests visibility on guides that John mentions.

**John's Response:**
- John suggests a model where the performance team offers various levels of service (Manage, Support, Consult) which they are ready to apply to accessibility as well. He is concerned about the inefficiency of embedding a highly specialized tester in every team.
- Later, John agrees that accessibility should be an inherent part of the development process from the start.

**Varun's Input:**
- Varun advocates embedding accessibility testers in teams, making several points about specialization, continuous attention, agility, and division of focus.
- He articulates the importance of accessibility throughout the project phases in Waterfall methodologies.
- Additionally, Varun adds to John's meeting points by clarifying the roles and responsibilities of the ACoE and project teams in ensuring compliance.

**Opinion and Considerations:**

1. **Balancing Specialization and Efficiency:**
   - The cost and efficiency concerns John raises about embedding A11y Testers are valid. A hybrid approach might be more effective, where there is a dedicated A11y expert within larger projects or squads with frequent A11y testing needs, while smaller or less A11y-focused teams might share a resource or utilize consultative support.

2. **Training and Upskilling:**
   - To bridge the skills gap John mentions, a comprehensive training program to upskill existing testers and developers on accessibility may reduce the need for specialized testers and embed the needed expertise directly within agile squads.

3. **Collaborative Approach:**
   - The proposed collaboration between the NFT and ACoE teams seems promising for providing a consistent level of quality in accessibility across different projects. As John suggests, all parties should work out an engagement process to prevent friction.

4. **Process Integration and Responsibility:**
   - As Varun states, the responsibility for accessibility should be clearly established within each project team, but with support from the ACoE. This is vital to ensure that accessibility is not an afterthought but a core aspect of SDLC.

5. **Deferment Process:**
   - Varun's point on a "deferment request" is an important safety net to avoid go-live delays. However, it should be carefully managed to ensure it does not become a bypass for compliance.

6. **Building an Inclusive Culture:**
   - The conversation aligns with the broader need to build an inclusive culture within the organization where accessibility is a shared value and not just a technical checklist.

Overall, it’s crucial to find a cost-effective yet robust way to integrate consistent, high-quality accessibility testing into both agile and waterfall methodologies. Such a practice would not only ensure legal compliance and safeguard against reputational risk but also support the larger goal of inclusive software for all users. Each stakeholder brings a fundamental perspective, and continued collaboration is key to developing a sustainable accessibility strategy moving forward.
Based on the above conversation thread and your analysis, provide me process to handle the attestations.

Handling attestations in the context of integrated accessibility within an organization involves creating a clear process that ensures every piece of work meets compliance standards before it's released. Here's a suggested process tailored to handle attestations based on the conversation thread:

**1. Pre-Development Planning and Assessment:**
   - Embed accessibility considerations and requirements from the ideation phase. This includes having accessibility experts involved in planning meetings to provide input on the scope and scale of accessibility needs.

**2. Accessibility Training and Upskilling:**
   - Invest in training for development teams to elevate their understanding of accessibility. This helps in ensuring that they can proactively design and code with compliance in mind.
   - Create a repository of resources, guidelines, and best practices accessible to all teams.

**3. Development and Testing Integration:**
   - For agile teams, include accessibility criteria as part of the "Definition of Done" for each sprint. Accessibility experts or trained team members should conduct ongoing testing.
   - For waterfall teams, ensure there are checkpoints at the end of each phase where accessibility is reviewed and approved before moving to the next phase.

**4. Attestation Process Implementation:**
   - Assign an Accessibility Champion within each squad/project to take responsibility for maintaining the accessibility checklist and ensuring all criteria are met.
   - Develop an accessibility scorecard that tracks compliance throughout the development life cycle.

**5. Regular Audits and Escalations:**
   - Conduct periodic audits to validate that the work complies with accessibility standards.
   - If issues are found, there should be a clear process for escalating and addressing these before release, with collaboration from ACoE.

**6. Accessibility Review Pre-Release:**
   - Prior to release, have a formal Accessibility Review where artifacts (e.g., test results, compliance documentation) are submitted for review by ACoE to issue an attestation.
   - Define clear timelines for when projects should submit their accessibility artifacts, considering the agility needed for continuous delivery models.

**7. Attestation and Approval:**
   - If the accessibility artifacts meet the required standards, the ACoE issues an attestation approving the project for release.
   - If the project falls short, the attestation is withheld, and the team is advised on the necessary corrective actions before attestation can be issued.

**8. Post-Release Monitoring:**
   - Have mechanisms in place for users to provide feedback on accessibility post-release.
   - Monitor accessibility of live applications to ensure ongoing compliance and update tests and practices based on real-world use and feedback.

**9. Deferment Request Process:**
   - Develop a process for deferment requests that contain:
     - Criteria that justify a deferment.
     - A timeline for when the issues will be addressed.
     - Approval and oversight mechanisms for any request.

**10. Continuous Improvement:**
   - Incorporate lessons learned into continuous improvement initiatives for both the development process and the attestation process itself.

Each step of this process requires collaboration, communication, and commitment from all involved parties—the project teams, NFT, ACoE, and other stakeholders. It's also important that the process is transparent and well-documented so all members involved understand their roles, responsibilities, and the importance of accessibility in the organization's mission and values.
In the above context, what is the meaning of "attestations"?

In the context of the conversation transcript provided, "attestations" refer to the formal declarations or acknowledgments made by a team (often the project team or the Accessibility Centre of Excellence (ACoE)) that their product, service, or system has been tested for accessibility and meets certain standards or criteria. These attestations are essentially a form of assurance that the necessary accessibility checks and processes have been properly executed and that the resulting product is compliant with required accessibility guidelines or laws, such as the Web Content Accessibility Guidelines (WCAG).

These attestations are essential in:
- Certifying that due diligence has been done regarding accessibility compliance.
- Demonstrating the organization's commitment to providing accessible products to all users, including those with disabilities.
- Serving as a checkpoint before product release to ensure that any accessibility issues have been identified and rectified.
- Potentially being part of the governance and risk management process where compliance with accessibility standards is audited and monitored.

The attestation process usually involves a thorough review of the accessibility testing results and other relevant documentation to confirm compliance. If a product passes this review, the attestation acts as an official record that accessibility standards have been met and that the product can be released. If the product does not meet the required standards, the attestation will not be granted until issues are resolved.
What does fully compliant means in terms of accessibility?

In terms of accessibility, being "fully compliant" means that a product, service, or website meets all the specified requirements of a particular set of accessibility standards or guidelines. This demonstrates that the necessary steps have been taken to ensure the content is accessible to all users, including those with disabilities such as visual, auditory, motor, cognitive, or other impairments.

The most widely recognized standards for web accessibility are the Web Content Accessibility Guidelines (WCAG), published by the World Wide Web Consortium's (W3C) Web Accessibility Initiative (WAI). The guidelines are broken down into different levels of compliance:

1. **Level A (Minimum Level of Compliance)**: The most basic web accessibility features must be in place to satisfy this level. Without meeting Level A criteria, it will be very hard for some users to access web content.

2. **Level AA (Mid Range)**: Addresses the most common barriers for disabled users. Many laws and policies require at least Level AA compliance.

3. **Level AAA (Highest Level)**: The most stringent level of web accessibility that covers the widest range of users and situations. However, not all content can meet this level due to the nature of the content and the degree of accessibility that would be required.

Being "fully compliant" often refers to meeting all the criteria set out at a certain conformance level (usually AA, for most regulations). This includes a range of requirements including but not limited to:
- Text alternatives for non-text content (alt-tags for images).
- Captions and other alternatives for multimedia.
- Content presented in a way that can be perceived by all users (for example, not by color alone).
- Functionality available from a keyboard.
- Allowing users to control time limits on their reading or interaction.
- Not designing content in a way that is known to cause seizures.
- Providing navigational aids for finding content and determining where the user is.
- Ensuring text and background have enough contrast.
- Making forms, tables, and other content structurally sound and clearly labeled.
- Ensuring compatibility with various browsers, assistive technologies, and other user agents.

To conclude, being "fully compliant" means that the necessary accessibility-related adjustments have been identified and implemented to meet the requirements of the chosen guidelines across all relevant parts and features of the product or content, ensuring usability for all individuals regardless of disabilities.

Let's assume that there is a product with 100 accessibility defects having Critical, High, Medium and Low severity. Tell me what will comprises of Fully compliant, Partially compliant and Non-compliant.

Determining full compliance, partial compliance, or non-compliance with accessibility standards typically depends on addressing the severity and impact of the identified defects, as well as the criteria of the applicable accessibility guidelines, such as WCAG.

Let's establish a general framework based on WCAG conformance levels and the hypothetical defect severities:

### Fully Compliant:
A product is considered fully compliant when it has addressed all identified accessibility defects across all severity levels (Critical, High, Medium, and Low) in accordance with the target level of WCAG (e.g., AA):

- **Critical**: These are defects that make the product completely inaccessible or unusable for certain users. They must be fixed in order to claim any level of compliance.
- **High**: These defects significantly impede the ability of users to access important functionality or content and must be resolved.
- **Medium**: These defects may not block access but could hinder the overall ease of use or understanding for certain users. They should be corrected to ensure a fully inclusive experience.
- **Low**: These are considered minor issues that have a lower impact on the overall user experience but should still be fixed to achieve full compliance.

### Partially Compliant:
A product can be considered partially compliant if it has addressed the majority of the accessibility defects, particularly the Critical and High severity ones, but some Medium and Low defects remain unresolved. In some cases, partial compliance also refers to meeting all the Level A and most Level AA criteria but not all:

- **Critical and High**: All or most defects in these categories must be fixed to even consider partial compliance.
- **Medium and Low**: There might be some outstanding issues, but as long as they do not significantly hinder accessibility for most users, partial compliance can still be claimed.

### Non-Compliant:
A product falls into the non-compliant category when it has multiple unresolved accessibility defects, particularly those of Critical and High severity that prevent users with disabilities from accessing core functionality or content:

- **Critical and High**: If numerous defects in these categories are present and not remedied, the product cannot be considered compliant.
- **Medium and Low**: Many unresolved issues in these categories could also result in non-compliance, especially if they cumulatively lead to significant barriers for users with disabilities.

**Important Considerations:**
- Compliance is not always binary. Some organizations might consider a product partially compliant if all critical issues are resolved and a plan is in place to address remaining issues.
- Compliance is often an ongoing process; as digital products evolve, new accessibility challenges can arise, necessitating continuous monitoring and updating.
- Some regulatory, legal, or contractual requirements might influence the definition of compliance, necessitating strict adherence to certain standards.

Ultimately, the determination of full, partial, or non-compliance will depend on the specific accessibility guidelines being referenced (such as WCAG 2.1 Level AA), the nature and impact of the defects, and the regulatory context in which the product is used.
Tell me in terms of percentage.

Quantifying compliance with accessibility standards in terms of percentage can be complex, as it's not typically how standards like WCAG define compliance. WCAG, for example, is structured around meeting specific success criteria rather than achieving a percentage score. Accessibility is generally seen as binary—either you meet the success criteria (compliant) or you do not (non-compliant).

However, for the sake of explanation and a simplified understanding, we can attempt to translate the notion of compliance into percentages based on the hypothetical scenario of defects you provided.

### Fully Compliant:
100% - All 100 accessibility defects (Critical, High, Medium, Low) have been resolved.

### Partially Compliant:
This can vary a great deal depending on which and how many issues have been addressed. Here's an example of one way to illustrate partial compliance:

- 90-99% - All Critical and High severity issues are resolved, with only a few Medium or Low severity issues remaining.
- 70-89% - All Critical issues are resolved, most High severity issues are resolved, and varying numbers of Medium and Low severity issues are outstanding.
- 50-69% - Most Critical issues are resolved, some High severity issues are outstanding, and numerous Medium and Low severity issues remain.

### Non-Compliant:
0-49% - Many Critical and High severity issues are unresolved, leading to significant accessibility barriers. This might also include having any number of unresolved Critical issues, as they often present complete barriers to use for individuals with disabilities.

It's important to underline that this percentage framework is hypothetical and not an official measure used in accessibility compliance. In practice, a product is typically considered non-compliant if it has any unresolved Critical defects, regardless of the number of other defects that have been fixed. Consequently, assessing compliance requires a detailed analysis of each issue's impact on the user experience, rather than a simple tally.
Explain the above.

The above explanation attempts to depict accessibility compliance in terms of percentages, which is not a standard approach in real-world accessibility practices. Traditional accessibility guidelines like WCAG focus on meeting specific qualitative criteria rather than reducing non-compliance to a quantitative measure such as percentage. Nonetheless, for illustrative purposes, let’s explain this further:

When discussing accessibility defects, they are typically categorized based on the severity of their impact on users with disabilities:

- **Critical Defects**: These represent issues that entirely block users with disabilities from accessing or using key functionality. Critical issues must be resolved for a product to be considered accessible at any level of compliance.

- **High Severity Defects**: These are significant issues that greatly impede the user experience for users with disabilities, potentially preventing them from completing essential tasks. Resolving these is also crucial for achieving compliance.

- **Medium Severity Defects**: These defects may cause inconvenience or inefficiency for users with disabilities but can generally be worked around. Although they should be fixed to improve the user experience, they are less of a barrier than Critical or High severity defects.

- **Low Severity Defects**: These are minor issues that are likely not to severely impact the user's ability to use the application. While addressing these is part of good practice and ensures a higher level of conformity, they are the lowest priority for immediate action.

The percentage values are a hypothetical representation to convey the extent to which an application meets accessibility standards based on how many of these issues are fixed across different severity levels.

### Fully Compliant (100%)

An application is fully compliant if it successfully addresses 100% of the accessibility defects—Critical, High, Medium, and Low. This implies that the application has no known barriers that would prevent users with disabilities from accessing any feature or content.

### Partially Compliant (Ranges Between 50%-99%)

A product is only partially compliant if it has unresolved accessibility issues. The percentages—(50-69%, 70-89%, 90-99%)—are arbitrary and used only for illustration. They represent the relative completion of efforts to resolve accessibility defects, with respect to the urgency and impact of those issues:

- **90-99%**: The application has addressed nearly all issues, with only a negligible number of low-impact defects outstanding.

- **70-89%**: Most critical and high-severity issues are fixed, but several medium and potentially some high-severity issues remain.

- **50-69%**: The application has addressed a significant number of issues, yet a substantial number of high and medium-severity issues haven't been resolved.

### Non-Compliant (0%-49%)

An application is non-compliant if it fails to address a large portion of identified accessibility barriers, particularly those with Critical or High severity. The product has fundamental flaws that prevent it from being accessible and usable for people with disabilities. Even if it addresses 49% or fewer defects, the presence of unresolved Critical defects alone would deem the application non-compliant.

Again, this method of depicting compliance lacks the nuance that real-world accessibility compliance requires. Indeed, resolving all Critical defects might indeed be more important for baseline compliance than addressing a certain percentage of lower-severity defects. Thus, while these percentage ranges can provide a rough guide to how many issues might typically be outstanding at different levels of compliance, actual accessibility compliance determination is based on the qualitative assessment of whether the necessary WCAG criteria have been met to a stipulated conformance level (typically Level AA for legal requirements).

Let’s assume that there is a product with 100 accessibility defects having Critical, High, Medium and Low severity. Tell me what will comprises of Fully compliant, Partially compliant and Non-compliant.

Using the hypothetical situation of a product with 100 accessibility defects across different severity levels, here's how "Fully Compliant," "Partially Compliant," and "Non-Compliant" might be conceptualized:

### Fully Compliant
The product would be considered fully compliant only if all 100 accessibility defects are fixed, no matter their severity. This means that:

- All Critical defects have been resolved.
- All High severity defects have been resolved.
- All Medium severity defects have been resolved.
- All Low severity defects have been resolved.

In the context of WCAG, full compliance would typically mean that all success criteria for the chosen level of conformance (A, AA, or AAA) have been met without exception.

### Partially Compliant
A product would be considered partially compliant if some but not all accessibility defects are fixed. The degree of partial compliance can vary widely based on which and how many defects remain unresolved. For example:

- All Critical defects and most High severity defects are fixed, but some Medium and Low severity defects are outstanding.
- All Critical and High severity defects may be fixed, with a significant number of Medium and Low severity defects outstanding.

For many organizations, satisfying all Level A and most AA success criteria may be considered a state of partial compliance, with the goal being to address any outstanding defects in AA and any applicable AAA criteria.

### Non-Compliant
The product would be deemed non-compliant if it still has outstanding Critical and High severity defects that significantly impair accessibility for users with disabilities, such as:

- Unresolved Critical defects that prevent usage of the product for one or more groups of users with disabilities.
- A substantial number of High severity defects that severely limit the functionality or content.
- Even a combination of numerous Medium and Low severity defects that together create significant barriers.

In this case, the product does not meet the basic accessibility requirements, and its defects pose clear obstacles to users with disabilities.

Remember, achieving compliance is not about the number of defects alone—it's about the impact those defects have on the ability of users to access and use the product according to established accessibility guidelines. The severity of defects and their impact on the user experience determine the level of compliance. All Critical and High severity issues generally require fixing to achieve any level of compliance, while resolving Medium and Low severity issues is necessary for full compliance.

Here is the engagement process I created for collaboration between Accessibility Centre of Excellence and Non-functional testing:
Inclusive Development Lifecycle Integration:
1. Early Engagement: NFT associates acting as accessibility champions are brought in early during the development lifecycle to promote "shift left" approach on accessibility.
2. Accessibility Evangelism: NFT associates acts as champions of accessibility in their project teams, promoting best practices and standards set forth by ACoE.

Now I received the following comment on the above process:
This section makes me think we should say something about inclusive design. I think much of this is on Struan's control, but it's not a given. I'm not sure all project teams know they need to do inclusive design and may not have designers in their team. I wonder how we handle this? I don't think it is one for this document though, but don't know where it fits. 

Please analyze this engagement process along with the comment and provide your analysis. Also, tell me what should I reply in response.

The engagement process for collaboration between the Accessibility Centre of Excellence (ACoE) and Non-functional Testing (NFT) teams as outlined in the "Inclusive Development Lifecycle Integration" is a good starting point. It suggests a proactive approach to accessibility, with a focus on integrating accessibility considerations early in the development cycle ("shift left") and creating accessibility champions within NFT who promote best practices and act as advocates ("Accessibility Evangelism") within project teams.

### Analysis of the Engagement Process:
1. **Early Engagement**: Including NFT associates early in the development lifecycle ensures that accessibility is considered from the very beginning of a project, rather than as an afterthought. This approach has the benefit of potentially reducing the need for rework and helping to identify accessibility barriers when they're easier (and often less expensive) to address.

2. **Accessibility Evangelism**: Having champions in the project teams who advocate for accessibility and best practices helps in creating a culture of inclusiveness and ensures that team members are constantly reminded of the importance of incorporating accessibility. Champions play a crucial role in peer education and in the enforcement of ACoE guidelines.

### Analysis of the Comment and Recommendations:
The comment suggests that while the engagement process for NFT is heading in the right direction, there is an underlying concern that not all project teams may be equipped to undertake inclusive design due to a possible lack of awareness or lack of design expertise within the team. Inclusive design is a framework for creating products that are usable by the widest range of people, considering all potential users regardless of disability or other factors.

**Points to consider**:

- **Inclusive Design Education**: It's possible that not every project team member is familiar with the principles of inclusive design. As such, there might be a need for educational programs or inclusion workshops that are led by the ACoE to raise awareness and promote the mindset throughout the organization.

- **Multidisciplinary Approach**: Inclusive design typically requires a multidisciplinary approach. If some teams do not have designers experienced in accessibility, it might be necessary to provide access to such expertise, whether through centralized design resources or through additional training for existing team members.

- **Role of ACoE**: The ACoE might need to take an active role in guiding design processes, setting standards, and ensuring that resources for inclusive design are available to all teams that might need them.

### Suggested Reply in Response:
"Thank you for your valuable feedback. You've raised a critical point about ensuring inclusive design practices are embedded across all project teams. It's true that not every team has direct access to designers with a background in accessibility or may not be fully aware that inclusive design is part of their remit. To address this, we could consider the following steps:

1. Expand our training offerings to include inclusive design principles, led by the ACoE, and ensure that all appropriate team members can participate.
2. Assess the current design capabilities within our project teams and identify gaps where additional support and expertise may be necessary.
3. Explore the feasibility of creating a resource pool or support system within ACoE that can provide expert guidance on inclusive design on an as-needed basis for projects lacking in this area.

We acknowledge that this topic may extend beyond the scope of the current document. However, it is crucial for the overall success of our accessibility initiatives. Perhaps we can initiate a separate document or guidelines on inclusive design practices to augment our engagement process, ensuring that this essential component receives the appropriate focus throughout our organization."

This response acknowledges the concern, suggests practical next steps to address the gap, and opens up the dialogue for further discussion on how to incorporate inclusive design into the company's culture effectively.

You are an accessibility expert. I am trying to enhance "Advance Framework" with accessibility. Here is the definition of Advance Framework:
Advance Framework is based on the principle of Minimum Viable Product MVP (key) document that most projects require and is tailored based on the nature and scale of the programme / project.

The Advance waterfall methodology has seven key stages:

1. Idea:
a. Identify a regulatory requirement, or a good investment opportunity.
b. Budget Approval
c. Agree priority against other commitments.
d. Ready to mobilise.

2. Shape:
a. Initiate work to explore solution options.
b. Engage leads or specialist resources to develop Outline Business Case.

Your task is to analyse the 7 stages of Advance Framework and integrate accessibility wherever applicable.

Integrating accessibility within the Advance Framework requires consideration at every stage of the waterfall methodology to ensure that the products or services are usable by people of all abilities. Below, I will provide a breakdown of how accessibility can be embedded within each of the seven stages:

### 1. Idea:
- **a. Regulatory Requirement/Investment Opportunity**: Assess the project for any specific accessibility regulations (e.g., ADA, Section 508, WCAG standards) it needs to comply with. Recognize accessibility as a worthwhile investment, as it expands the potential market reach.
- **b. Budget Approval**: Ensure that the budget includes provisions for accessibility testing tools, expert resources, user testing with people who have disabilities, and potential remediation efforts.
- **c. Priority Agreement**: Factor in accessibility as a critical component, equally important as any other key business requirements.
- **d. Mobilization**: Plan to mobilize accessibility resources and build a team competent in creating accessible solutions from the start.

### 2. Shape:
- **a. Solution Exploration**: Incorporate accessibility considerations into all solution options, including technical and non-technical solutions.
- **b. Outline Business Case**: Develop the business case to include the benefits and necessity of creating an accessible product, highlighting legal compliance, market expansion, and brand enhancement.
- **c. Resource Request**: Ensure that the resources being requested include specialists in accessibility or a plan to upskill existing resources.

### 3. Define:
- **a. Option Assessment**: Verify that the selected go-forward solution is accessible or has a clear path to become accessible.
- **b. Interim Business Case**: Include accessibility metrics and tracking in the business case to guarantee project alignment with accessibility goals.
- **c. Resource Request**: Confirm that the requested resources are adequate to achieve the accessibility objectives outlined.

### 4. Design:
- **a. Producing Designs**: Integrate accessibility into the design process, ensuring that the people, process, and technology designs meet or exceed required accessibility standards.
- **b. Clarity and Unknowns**: Use part of this phase to conduct an accessibility audit to identify any potential barriers in the designs.
- **c. Business Case Approval**: The full business case should include a clear statement of how the design supports accessibility standards.
- **d. Resource Request**: Allocate resources specifically for completing accessibility testing and remediation.

### 5. Build & Test:
- **a. Solution Development**: Build accessibility testing into the regular testing regimen, covering both automated testing and manual expert evaluations.
- **b. Business and Service Readiness**: Validate that the organization's support and maintenance processes are accessible (e.g., help desk, documentation).
- **c. Implementation Plan**: Ensure the plan includes final accessibility checks and user testing with participants with disabilities.
- **d. Go/No Go Decision**: One of the criteria for the go decision should be the status of accessibility compliance.

### 6. Implement:
- **a. New Solution Implementation**: Oversee the deployment to ensure installation or implementation processes are accessible.
- **b. Business and Service Cutover**: Train support staff in handling accessibility queries and issues that might arise post-implementation.
- **c. Warranty Exit**: Plan for an accessibility evaluation during the warranty period to ensure compliance is maintained.
- **d. Closure Preparation**: Document accessibility features and known issues to ensure continuous improvement in future projects.

### 7. Run & Realise:
- **a. Project Activities**: Review accessibility compliance to confirm continued adherence to standards as part of project close-out activities.
- **b. BAU Operations**: Ensure that accessibility is a considered factor in maintenance and ongoing operations.
- **c. Benefit Realisation Ownership**: Assign owners for ongoing accessibility improvements and ensure they understand their responsibilities.
- **d. Success Celebration**: Recognize the achievement of creating an accessible product as an integral part of project success.

Embedding accessibility within each stage of the Advance Framework ensures that accessibility considerations are seamlessly integrated into the project lifecycle, rather than being an ad-hoc activity or retrospective adjustment. It should be part of the ethos and practice throughout the project, which will optimize the accessibility of the product and support the organization in achieving its legal, ethical, and business objectives.

Your task is to analyse the 7 stages of Advance Framework and try to enhance it with accessibility.

Enhancing the Advance Framework with accessibility involves integrating accessibility considerations and actions into each of the seven stages. Here’s what this might involve:

### 1. Idea:
- **a. Regulatory Requirement/Investment Opportunity**: Ensure that accessibility laws (ADA, Section 508, AODA, EN 301 549, etc.) are considered as part of regulatory compliance. View accessibility as an investment in market expansion and corporate social responsibility.
- **b. Budget Approval**: Secure a budget that includes funding for accessibility resources, tools, training, and consulting services.
- **c. Agree Priority**: Make accessibility a high priority from the outset, on par with other strategic initiatives within the organization.
- **d. Ready to Mobilise**: Plan to engage accessibility specialists at project kickoff to provide early guidance and ensure the MVP considers accessibility from the start.

### 2. Shape:
- **a. Explore Solution Options**: Include accessibility evaluation as part of exploring technical and non-technical solution options, promoting universal design principles.
- **b. Outline Business Case**: Develop a business case that articulates the benefits of an accessible solution, such as reaching a wider audience, improving brand reputation, and mitigating legal risks.
- **c. Formal Resource Request**: Request resources that have the necessary accessibility knowledge or include plans for training and development in accessibility skills.

### 3. Define:
- **a. Assess Options**: Ensure that the chosen solution prioritizes accessibility by including it in the selection criteria for technologies and platforms.
- **b. Interim Business Case**: Reflect the value of accessibility within the interim business case, including the cost implications of retrofitting vs. upfront integration.
- **c. Formal Resource Request**: In resource planning, explicitly include accessibility expertise across design, development, and testing.

### 4. Design:
- **a. Produce Designs**: Design with accessibility in mind across user interfaces, content, and interactions, adhering to WCAG guidelines and involving people with disabilities in user testing.
- **b. More Clarity, Fewer Unknowns**: Conduct accessibility risk assessments to foresee any potential issues and plan for their mitigation.
- **c. Full Business Case Approval**: Ensure the full business case accounts for the lifecycle cost of maintaining accessibility.
- **d. Formal Resource Request**: Plan for resources specialized in accessibility testing and necessary tools during the build and test stages.

### 5. Build & Test:
- **a. Build and Test**: Adopt an inclusive development approach. Implement automated and manual accessibility testing throughout development, including code reviews and user testing with participants with disabilities.
- **b. Business and Service Readiness**: Ensure all service touchpoints, including customer service and technical support, are accessible.
- **c. Implementation Plan**: Include steps for verifying accessibility in the pre-launch checklist.
- **d. Go/No Go Decision**: Include accessibility as part of the release criteria. No product should go live with significant unresolved accessibility issues.

### 6. Implement:
- **a. Implement New Solution**: Launch the solution with confirmed accessibility support, and plan for immediate remediation of any issues encountered upon deployment.
- **b. Business and Service Cutover**: Ensure transition and handover documents include details on the accessibility features and training for staff on their importance.
- **c. Warranty Exit**: Establish an accessibility warranty period to observe, rectify, and update any issues uncovered post-implementation.
- **d. Prepare for Closure**: Collect accessibility-related lessons learned to inform future projects.

### 7. Run & Realise:
- **a. Close-out**: Verify that all accessibility requirements have been met and document performance for compliance purposes and improvement opportunities.
- **b. Embed into BAU**: Ensure ongoing operations maintain accessibility through regular audits and updates.
- **c. Benefit Realisation**: Track and report on the broader benefits of accessibility, such as improved customer satisfaction and expanded audience reach.
- **d. Celebrate Success**: Highlight successful accessibility outcomes and recognize team contributions to building a more inclusive product.

By systematically integrating accessibility considerations into each stage of the Advance Framework, you create an inclusive process that promotes equality, enhances user experience for a broader audience, and aligns with best practices and legal requirements for accessibility.
